does: This paper presents an autonomous system that uses large language model-guided tree search to generate, evaluate, and optimize executable forecasting models for infectious diseases without manual expert curation. The system automatically discovers diverse methodological approaches and aggregates them into ensemble predictions. During prospective real-time evaluation across the 2025-2026 US respiratory season, it produced models for influenza, COVID-19, and respiratory syncytial virus that matched or outperformed CDC gold-standard human-curated ensemble forecasts out-of-sample. The system handled cold-start scenarios for RSV with limited historical data and demonstrated that log-scale distance metric optimization prevents reward hacking in the automated discovery process.
connects: This work builds on established traditions in disease forecasting that rely on expert-curated mechanistic and statistical models, as exemplified by CDC forecast hubs. It responds to the critical bottleneck that bespoke model development by specialized teams limits scalability to new pathogens, geographic granularity, and rapid deployment. The approach draws on recent advances in LLM-guided automated machine learning and neural architecture search, where language models direct computational exploration of algorithmic spaces. It also connects to ensemble forecasting methods that aggregate diverse models to improve prediction robustness, and to recent work on using LLMs for scientific discovery and code generation in quantitative domains.
enables: This automation framework opens pathways toward rapid deployment of forecasting systems for emerging pathogens and fine-grained spatial predictions without requiring specialized epidemiological modeling teams. The system enables exploration of methodological diversity that human experts might not discover, potentially improving forecast accuracy through automated ensemble construction. Future work can extend this to other disease systems, integrate real-time data streams more flexibly, and investigate how autonomous discovery might identify novel mechanistic insights about disease transmission. The approach also suggests broader applications of LLM-guided search for scientific model discovery and raises questions about automating expert judgment in public health decision-making at scale.
